text: We routinely use injections of liposomal bupivacaine, which has provided excellent pain relief rather than thoracic epidurals.
Dissection begins by opening the pleura overlying the esophagus posterior to the lung.
This dissection is continued cephalad to expose the inferior aspect of the bronchus intermedius and to expose the bronchus to the upper lobe as it takes off just beneath the azagus vein.
At this point the dissection should be very similar to that for a right upper lobectomy
with exposure of the right upper lobe takeoff and the branch point to the bronchus intermedius.
Next, the pulmonary artery is identified in the major fissure.
Again, the pleura is opened here.
Pulsations can be used to guide dissection in this area, but usually the pulmonary artery is very close to the surface.

Once the pulmonary artery has been identified, this plane is used to connect with the prior dissection posteriorly such that the major fissure can be completed with a firing of the endo-GIA stapler in this case.
This will minimize air leaks.
Having completed the major fissure, vascular structures are then identified.
Next to the posterior ascending branch of the pulmonary artery, the posterior segmental vein is readily identified.
This should be the first structure which is encountered in the fissure during the course of this dissection.
This is encircled as in standard manner and divided using a vascular load of the stapler.

Once the vein is divided, it is sometimes helpful to use the divided vein to provide some traction to allow for the posterior ascending branch of the pulmonary artery to be next encircled and divided using the stapler.
Once these two vascular structures are divided, the bronchus is now exposed and dissected

Dissection of lymph nodes facilitates identification of branch points, and here a level 11 lymph node is retrieved between the branch point of the right upper lobe bronchus and the bronchus intermedius.

After inspection of the bronchus, bronchoscopy with a clamp placed on the segmental bronchus is a helpful safety measure prior to stapling the bronchus to ensure patency to the remaining anterior and apical segments if conventional anatomy is found.
